questions.question: The Harmful and Harassing Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 (the 'Coco Act') commenced in Ireland on 9 February 2021. What was the primary purpose of the Act?

options.A: The Act was designed exclusively to address cyberbullying of children — it does not apply to communications involving adults
options.B: The Act was designed to address technology-facilitated abuse, including the non-consensual sharing of intimate images ('revenge porn') and the sending of grossly offensive electronic communications; it criminalised conduct that was not adequately captured by the pre-existing law and provided specific protections for victims of online harm
options.C: The Act was designed solely to amend the Defamation Act 2009 to extend its protections to online communications
options.D: The Act created a new regulatory regime for social media platforms — its primary focus is platform liability rather than individual criminal conduct

questions.explanation: The Harmful and Harassing Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 (HHROA 2020), commonly known as the 'Coco Act' (named after Coco's Law following the campaign of Nicole Fox Fenlon's family), was enacted to address technology-facilitated abuse that was not adequately covered by existing criminal law. The Act's primary focus is: (1) the non-consensual sharing of intimate images — 'revenge porn' — under s.2; (2) the sending of grossly offensive electronic communications under s.4; and (3) victim anonymity protections under s.5. The Act also amended s.10 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 to extend the harassment offence to indirect communications. The Act commenced on 9 February 2021. Option A incorrectly limits the Act to children — it applies to adults. Option C incorrectly characterises the Act as a defamation amendment. Option D incorrectly identifies platform regulation as the primary purpose.

questions.question: Section 2 of the Harmful and Harassing Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 creates an offence relating to the non-consensual distribution of intimate images. What is the maximum sentence for this offence?
questions.explanation: Section 2 of the HHROA 2020 creates the offence of distributing, publishing, or threatening to distribute or publish an intimate image of another person without their consent (or where consent was obtained by deception). The maximum sentence for this offence is 7 years imprisonment on indictment. This reflects the gravity of the harm caused by non-consensual intimate image sharing — an offence that can cause devastating, lasting harm to victims including psychological harm, reputational damage, and interference with employment and relationships. The 7-year maximum places this offence in the category of serious indictable offences. FE-1 candidates must know the maximum sentence: 7 years. Options A and B state incorrect lower maximums. Option D incorrectly states 10 years.

questions.question: Section 4 of the Harmful and Harassing Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 creates an offence of sending a grossly offensive communication. What elements must be proved for this offence?

options.A: The prosecution must prove that the communication caused actual psychiatric harm to the recipient — without evidence of clinical harm, the offence cannot be established
options.B: The prosecution must prove that the accused sent a communication that was grossly offensive, false, or indecent, with the intention of causing harm to the recipient or with recklessness as to whether harm would be caused; the communication must reach the recipient or be likely to be seen by them
options.C: The offence requires proof that the communication was sent from an identifiable account — anonymous communications cannot constitute the offence
options.D: The prosecution must prove that the accused sent at least five such communications — a single grossly offensive message is insufficient

questions.explanation: Section 4 of the HHROA 2020 creates the offence of sending a communication that is grossly offensive, indecent, obscene, or false. The key elements are: (1) the sending of such a communication; (2) the intention to cause harm to the recipient, or recklessness as to whether harm is caused; and (3) the communication must be sent or published in a manner likely to be seen by the recipient or others. The maximum sentence for the s.4 offence is 2 years imprisonment (significantly lower than the s.2 intimate image offence, reflecting different levels of gravity). Option A incorrectly requires actual clinical harm — the offence is based on intention to harm or recklessness, not proven clinical injury. Option C incorrectly excludes anonymous communications — the identity of the sender does not determine the offence. Option D incorrectly requires a minimum number of communications — a single grossly offensive message can found the offence.

questions.question: What is the maximum sentence for the s.4 HHROA 2020 offence of sending a grossly offensive communication?

options.A: 6 months imprisonment on summary conviction only — the offence is not triable on indictment
options.D: 7 years imprisonment on indictment — the same as the intimate image offence

questions.explanation: The maximum sentence for the s.4 HHROA 2020 offence (sending a grossly offensive communication with intent to cause harm or recklessness as to harm) is 2 years imprisonment on indictment. This is lower than the s.2 intimate image offence (7 years), reflecting the difference in gravity between the two types of conduct. The sentencing framework under the HHROA 2020 creates a graduated set of penalties proportionate to the harm caused: s.2 intimate image offences (7 years); s.4 grossly offensive communications (2 years); s.5 victim anonymity failures (3 years). FE-1 candidates must distinguish between the maximum sentences for s.2 and s.4. Option A incorrectly limits the offence to summary conviction. Options C and D state incorrect, higher penalties.

questions.question: How did the Harmful and Harassing Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 amend s.10 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 (the harassment offence)?

options.A: The HHROA 2020 repealed s.10 NFOATP 1997 entirely and replaced it with a new, broader harassment offence
options.B: The HHROA 2020 amended s.10 NFOATP 1997 to extend the harassment offence to indirect communications — prior to the amendment, harassment under s.10 required direct communication with the victim; the amendment ensures that harassment through third parties or indirect means also falls within the offence
options.C: The HHROA 2020 amended s.10 NFOATP 1997 to increase the maximum sentence for harassment from 7 years to 10 years
options.D: The HHROA 2020 amended s.10 NFOATP 1997 to limit the harassment offence to online communications — traditional forms of harassment are now governed by a different provision

questions.explanation: The HHROA 2020 amended s.10 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 to extend the harassment offence to indirect communications. Prior to the amendment, there was a gap in the law — conduct amounting to harassment through indirect means (for example, sending communications to third parties intended to be passed on to the victim, or posting material about the victim on public platforms to reach them indirectly) was not clearly captured by s.10, which focused on direct communication. The HHROA 2020 amendment closes this gap by extending s.10 to indirect forms of harassing communication. This is an important extension because technology has created many new means of indirect harassment. Option A incorrectly states s.10 was repealed — it remains in force as amended. Option C incorrectly states the maximum sentence was changed. Option D incorrectly limits s.10 to online communications after the amendment.

questions.question: Emma shares intimate photographs of her former partner David on social media without his consent, intending to humiliate him. David suffers significant distress and reputational harm. Which offence under the HHROA 2020 has Emma potentially committed, and what is the maximum sentence?

options.A: Emma has committed the s.4 offence of sending a grossly offensive communication — maximum 2 years
options.B: Emma has committed the s.2 offence of distributing intimate images without consent — maximum 7 years on indictment; the conduct involves sharing intimate images without David's consent with the intention to cause him harm, which is precisely the conduct targeted by s.2
options.C: Emma has not committed any offence under the HHROA 2020 — the Act only applies to images shared privately between individuals, not images posted publicly on social media
options.D: Emma has committed both s.2 and s.4 offences simultaneously, and the sentences must run consecutively — minimum 9 years

questions.explanation: Emma has potentially committed the s.2 HHROA 2020 offence of distributing intimate images without consent. The elements are satisfied: (1) Emma distributed intimate photographs — images of a private or sexual nature; (2) without David's consent; (3) with the intention of causing harm (to humiliate him). The maximum sentence for the s.2 offence is 7 years on indictment. This scenario is the classic 'revenge porn' or non-consensual intimate image (NCII) scenario that the HHROA 2020 was designed to address. Posting to social media publicly is captured — the Act is not limited to private sharing. Option A incorrectly identifies the s.4 offence — the relevant provision for intimate images is s.2. Option C incorrectly excludes public posting — the Act covers distribution on any platform. Option D incorrectly imposes mandatory consecutive sentencing — no such rule exists under the Act.

questions.question: Section 5 of the Harmful and Harassing Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 provides protections for victims. What does s.5 primarily provide, and what is the maximum penalty for breach?

options.A: Section 5 provides that any person who publishes information identifying the victim of a s.2 or s.4 offence without the victim's consent commits a criminal offence — the maximum penalty for breach is 3 years imprisonment
options.B: Section 5 creates an automatic anonymity order for all victims — the court must order anonymity in every case and has no discretion to decline
options.C: Section 5 imposes a duty on social media platforms to remove reported intimate images within 24 hours — failure results in a fine of up to €250,000
options.D: Section 5 provides that victims can apply to the court for anonymity — but it is a discretionary provision and courts are not obliged to grant the order in every case

questions.explanation: Section 5 of the HHROA 2020 provides victim anonymity protections. The provision creates a criminal offence where a person publishes or broadcasts information that identifies (or is likely to identify) the victim of an offence under the Act without the victim's consent. The maximum penalty for breach of s.5 is 3 years imprisonment on indictment. This is an important protection — without it, victims of intimate image abuse could suffer a 'second victimisation' through media coverage identifying them. The s.5 penalty (3 years) sits between the s.4 penalty (2 years) and the s.2 penalty (7 years) in the sentencing framework. FE-1 candidates should note all three maximum penalties in the HHROA 2020 framework: s.2 (7 years), s.4 (2 years), s.5 (3 years). Option B incorrectly makes anonymity automatic and mandatory. Option C incorrectly describes a platform regulatory obligation. Option D mischaracterises the provision as purely discretionary.

questions.question: How does the offence under s.2 HHROA 2020 (non-consensual intimate image sharing) differ from the pre-existing offence of harassment under s.10 NFOATP 1997?

options.A: They are essentially the same offence — s.2 HHROA 2020 simply updated the maximum sentence for intimate image harassment
options.B: The offences are distinct: s.2 HHROA 2020 specifically targets the non-consensual distribution of intimate images and is complete on a single act of sharing (no persistence requirement); s.10 NFOATP 1997 harassment requires persistent conduct and targets a broader range of harassing behaviour; the s.2 offence addresses a specific form of technology-facilitated abuse that the harassment offence was not designed to capture; the maximum sentences also differ (s.2: 7 years; s.10: 7 years — but the conduct targeted is distinct)
options.C: Section 10 NFOATP 1997 has a higher maximum sentence than s.2 HHROA 2020 — harassment is considered more serious than intimate image sharing
options.D: Section 2 HHROA 2020 requires proof of persistent conduct over time — it differs from s.10 only in that it is specifically limited to intimate images

questions.explanation: Sections 2 HHROA 2020 and s.10 NFOATP 1997 are distinct offences that address different (though potentially overlapping) conduct. Key distinctions include: (1) Single act v. persistence — s.2 HHROA 2020 is complete on a single act of distributing an intimate image without consent; s.10 harassment requires persistent conduct (the offence is based on a course of behaviour, not a single incident); (2) Specific v. general — s.2 specifically targets intimate images; s.10 captures a broader range of harassing behaviour including surveillance, communication, and following; (3) Design — s.2 was specifically designed to address the phenomenon of 'revenge porn' and technology-facilitated image abuse which s.10 was not designed to capture when enacted in 1997; (4) Consent element — s.2 specifically focuses on the absence of consent to distribution. Both carry a maximum of 7 years on indictment, but address distinct social harms. Option A incorrectly conflates the offences. Option C incorrectly states s.10 has a higher maximum — both carry 7 years. Option D incorrectly attributes a persistence requirement to s.2.
